learning_outcomes.description: Understand the commercial publishing process from acquisition of a manuscript to final proofreading.
Identify and apply key drivers that may affect the publication and positive reception of manuscripts in the Australian market.
Understand and apply the conventions of structural editing.
Understand and apply the conventions of copyediting and proofreading.
Demonstrate sensitivity and attention to ethical practice when conveying editorial assessments and advice to authors and industry professionals.

assessments.task_description:
Throughout the semester, you will complete 4 in-class exercises: 2 copyediting tasks, 1 proofreading task, and one written reflection. Each exercise is worth 10% each for a maximum total of 40% of your course grade. The tasks are open book but secure: no digital devices are permitted.

Copyediting component (x2) – Weeks 8 and 10

You will annotate one fiction and one non-fiction text, suggesting ways to improve their clarity, consistency, and style. Your suggestions should relate to tone, readability, accuracy, and consistency, keeping in mind both genre conventions and reader expectations.

You will annotate and re-write parts of the text, providing a brief rationale for key editorial decisions and a completed style sheet (template provided) outlining stylistic choices you made during the edit.

You may refer to hardcopy notes and a dictionary.

Fryer reflection – Week 9

You will write a brief reflection of around 300 words as part of your Fryer Library field trip.

Proofreading component – Week 11

You will identify and correct errors (including spelling, punctuation, grammar, formatting, and typographical issues) in a sample of typeset text.

You must make these corrections using standard proofreading symbols, as practised in our seminars. You may refer to a dictionary and a hand-out listing the main proofreading marks.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
For this assignment, you will read one novel from the list of debut Australian titles provided on Blackboard (see the folder in the Assessment section). You will take on the role of an editor who has just read the manuscript and is preparing to present it at an acquisitions meeting. Your task is to write a persuasive publishing proposal (800–1000 words) using the acquisitions proposal proforma provided on Blackboard.

In the lead-up to submitting your proposal, you will participate in an in-class acquisitions meeting, where you will pitch your book to a group of your classmates, respond to questions about your proposal, and engage in discussion by asking thoughtful questions of your peers. You should use this feedback to refine your proposal before submission, and you should explain any changes you've made based on feedback in the reflection section of the assignment template. The reflection section is worth 5% of the 25% total for this assignment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit the proposal after the meeting via TurnItIn through Blackboard
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
For this assignment, you will choose one of the manuscripts from Blackboard to work on throughout the semester.

Your task is to write a structural report of around 2,500 to 3,000 words, offering clear, constructive, and comprehensive feedback to the author. While you may decide on the overall structure and format of your report, it should reflect your editorial judgement and the specific needs of the manuscript you’ve selected. Your report should identify both strengths and weaknesses, provide specific suggestions where appropriate, and maintain a supportive and professional tone throughout.

High-quality reports are typically thoughtful, consistent, and genuinely helpful to the author. You are expected to express yourself clearly, and your report should be carefully proofread and mechanically flawless.

To guide your approach, you should review and evaluate one or more of the sample structural reports available on Blackboard using the provided criteria sheet—consider where you can improve and what best practices you can adopt in your own work.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Welcome to WRIT7070 Editing the Manuscript

Welcome to the course: this seminar will give you an overview of WRIT7070, an opportunity to introduce yourself to your classmates, and a chance to dive into some activities including our first round of hands-on copyediting practice and a skills matrix.


Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 1: Welcome to WRIT7070 Editing the Manuscript
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: Welcome to WRIT7070 Editing the Manuscript Welcome to the course: this seminar will give you an overview of WRIT7070, an opportunity to introduce yourself to your classmates, and a chance to dive into some activities including our first round of hands-on copyediting practice and a skills matrix.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Acquisitions

This seminar includes a guest lecture from UQP's new publishing director. It covers paths to publication, the role of the acquisitions editor, and issues including genre trends and market cycles, diversity and representation in acquisitions, and questions around commercial vs literary value. We'll discuss the acquisitions assignment and do some more hands-on copyediting practice.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 2: Acquisitions This seminar includes a guest lecture from UQP's new publishing director. It covers paths to publication, the role of the acquisitions editor, and issues including genre trends and market cycles, diversity and representation in acquisitions, and questions around commercial vs literary value. We'll discuss the acquisitions assignment and do some more hands-on copyediting practice.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: Reading week

You can use this week to work on your acquisitions proposal draft.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: Reading week You can use this week to work on your acquisitions proposal draft.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Foundations of editorial practice

This seminar will cover guiding principles and professional practices for editors and give you an opportunity to gain feedback on your draft acquisitions proposal. Participating in this seminar is necessary for you to be able to complete the 5% reflective component of the acquisitions proposal.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4: Foundations of editorial practice This seminar will cover guiding principles and professional practices for editors and give you an opportunity to gain feedback on your draft acquisitions proposal. Participating in this seminar is necessary for you to be able to complete the 5% reflective component of the acquisitions proposal.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Structural editing – the basics

This seminar covers some of the basic principles and practices associated with structurally editing a full-length fiction or non-fiction manuscript. We'll be doing some more hands-on editing practice. (Remember your assignment is due this Friday by 4.00 pm.)
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 5: Structural editing – the basics This seminar covers some of the basic principles and practices associated with structurally editing a full-length fiction or non-fiction manuscript. We'll be doing some more hands-on editing practice. (Remember your assignment is due this Friday by 4.00 pm.)

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Structural editing – Plot, pace, and story beats

This seminar focuses on some of the most crucial principles of structural editing: how narrative elements function at the manuscript level. You'll learn about ways to assess plot development, pacing, story beats, and narrative structure, identifying strengths and weaknesses while developing strategies to improve coherence, momentum, and reader engagement. We'll work through some more hands-on editing practice to help prepare you for upcoming assessment.
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 6: Structural editing – Plot, pace, and story beats
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 6: Structural editing – Plot, pace, and story beats This seminar focuses on some of the most crucial principles of structural editing: how narrative elements function at the manuscript level. You'll learn about ways to assess plot development, pacing, story beats, and narrative structure, identifying strengths and weaknesses while developing strategies to improve coherence, momentum, and reader engagement. We'll work through some more hands-on editing practice to help prepare you for upcoming assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Structural editing – Characters, setting, theme

This seminar explores structural editing techniques for analysing and strengthening characters, setting, and theme. You'll consider how these elements contribute to an effective narrative, learning to identify inconsistencies, deepen characterisation, enhance world-building, and clarify thematic development to create more cohesive and compelling manuscripts. Again, we'll do some hands-on editing practice to help prepare you for upcoming assessment.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7:  Structural editing – Characters, setting, theme This seminar explores structural editing techniques for analysing and strengthening characters, setting, and theme. You'll consider how these elements contribute to an effective narrative, learning to identify inconsistencies, deepen characterisation, enhance world-building, and clarify thematic development to create more cohesive and compelling manuscripts. Again, we'll do some hands-on editing practice to help prepare you for upcoming assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Fundamentals of copyediting and proofreading

This seminar covers the fundamentals of copyediting, fact-checking, and proofreading – focusing on accuracy, clarity, consistency, and style. You'll learn more about key copyediting techniques, editorial conventions, and verification practices, developing skills in identifying errors, maintaining editorial standards, and ensuring the reliability and professionalism of written texts.

You'll complete your first in-class editing task worth 10%.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 8: Fundamentals of copyediting and proofreading This seminar covers the fundamentals of copyediting, fact-checking, and proofreading – focusing on accuracy, clarity, consistency, and style. You'll learn more about key copyediting techniques, editorial conventions, and verification practices, developing skills in identifying errors, maintaining editorial standards, and ensuring the reliability and professionalism of written texts. You'll complete your first in-class editing task worth 10%.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Fryer Library field trip

You'll visit the Fryer Library and have the opportunity to view some of its collection, including archived manuscripts and editorial notes. You'll need to write and submit a short reflection as part of this field trip.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 9: Fryer Library field trip You'll visit the Fryer Library and have the opportunity to view some of its collection, including archived manuscripts and editorial notes. You'll need to write and submit a short reflection as part of this field trip.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: Writing a structural report

This seminar focuses on how to prepare structural editorial reports. You'll learn more about how to evaluate a manuscript’s strengths and weaknesses, organise editorial observations, and communicate recommendations effectively. Our focus is on producing constructive feedback and developing actionable strategies to support manuscript revision.

You'll complete your second in-class editing task worth 10%.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 10: Writing a structural report This seminar focuses on how to prepare structural editorial reports. You'll learn more about how to evaluate a manuscript’s strengths and weaknesses, organise editorial observations, and communicate recommendations effectively. Our focus is on producing constructive feedback and developing actionable strategies to support manuscript revision. You'll complete your second in-class editing task worth 10%.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: The editor–author relationship

In this seminar, you'll have the opportunity to hear from a panel of authors and editors about their experiences of participating in the process of bringing a book to publication.

You'll also complete your third in-class editing task worth 10%. (This task focuses on proofreading.)
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 11: The editor–author relationship In this seminar, you'll have the opportunity to hear from a panel of authors and editors about their experiences of participating in the process of bringing a book to publication. You'll also complete your third in-class editing task worth 10%. (This task focuses on proofreading.)

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Editing across genres and contexts

This seminar will focus on editing as a profession, including desktop publishing skills.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 12: Editing across genres and contexts This seminar will focus on editing as a profession, including desktop publishing skills.

learning_activities.topic:
Reading week

This is a reading week. Please use this time to focus on writing your structural report, which is due on Monday 9 November by 4.00 pm.
